data.title: SentinelOne: The Market Is Wrong Here
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc., "S1" (S), may be one of the most deeply overlooked and misunderstood stocks in the market today. In fact, if there is one company that reminds me of Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) before it went on its 20x rampage, it's S1.

Of course, S1 is not a monopoly like Palantir, and I'm not saying that it will 10x or 20x in the next 1-2 years. Nonetheless, when Palantir was just transitioning from an unprofitable company to a modestly profitable one, its stock got no respect, much like S1 is getting no respect right now.

I pounded the table on Palantir when it was still sometimes missing EPS estimates by pennies. Its stock was still around the $6-8 range then. I feel that S1 is in a similar position. While it's still transitioning to a profitable enterprise, S1 is a top contender in the cybersecurity space and is also an AI leader in the segment.

Also, Palantir didn't always trade at 80 or 100 times sales. In its depressed pre-profitability days, it traded as low as about 6 times sales. Incidentally, S1 trades slightly below 6 times this year's expected sales and, remarkably, below 5 times next year's sales estimates.

S1's Earnings Could Skyrocket

Despite some negative perceptions about S1's earnings potential, S1 has only missed EPS estimates once (by one cent) since going public about five years ago. In fact, the consensus TTM EPS estimate was $0.07, yet S1 reported $0.10, posting a 43% outperformance rate over the estimates. The consensus EPS estimate is for $0.25 in the next four quarters. So, even if we incorporate a modest 20% EPS outperformance rate, we could see S1 deliver $0.30 in the next 12 months.

Nonetheless, the market looks ahead. Therefore, we should consider fiscal 2027 (2026) EPS estimates. The consensus EPS estimate is for $0.33, yet higher-end estimates range to over $0.50.

Applying a modest 20% outperformance rate to the $0.33 EPS estimate implies that S1 could earn about $0.40 in EPS next year. This dynamic puts S1's forward P/E ratio at around 40, relatively inexpensive for a high-growth AI software company just transitioning into a profitable enterprise.

However, to put S1's ridiculously low valuation into an even more accurate perspective, we can look at S1's price-to-sales ratio. Also, let's compare it to the other prominent cybersecurity players in the industry. This metric is key in my view, as S1 is primarily focused on growth over profitability at this stage, potentially making its P/S ratio a better valuation metric than its forward P/E ratio here. Forward P/S Ratios (Next Year's):

  • SentinelOne: 4.9.
  • Zscaler, Inc. (ZS): 11.
  • Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW): 11.
  • CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD): 18.

The Takeaway: S1 Is Just Too Cheap Here

We see that S1 is at least twice as cheap as its primary competitors. Technically, it could double in price and still be less expensive than Zscaler and Palo Alto Networks. Moreover, even if its price tripled, it would still be cheaper than CrowdStrike on a price-to-sales basis.

This dynamic could make sense if S1 had a faltering sales growth rate trajectory or had systemic issues with profitability or if there was something else plaguing the company. Yet, the opposite may be true. S1 has excellent sales growth potential; it could become considerably more profitable quicker than anticipated, and there don't appear to be any systemic issues at S1.

S1 Has Excellent Growth Potential Ahead

S1 is expected to post 20-30% revenue growth in the next five years or so. Moreover, it could maintain a very healthy 15-20% sales growth in the years beyond 2030. In comparison, Palo Alto's growth may only be about 10-15% in future years. Yet, its P/S multiple is more than 100% higher than S1's.

Zscaler's sales growth may be about S1's or potentially even slightly slower. Nonetheless, it too enjoys a P/S multiple of over 100% higher than S1. The only company that may achieve higher sales growth in this group, in my view, is CrowdStrike, but its P/S multiple is about 270% above S1's, making SentinelOne's valuation extremely attractive here.

Earnings Could Increase Faster Than Expected

The consensus EPS estimates imply that S1 can achieve a very robust 40-60% EPS growth rate in the next several years. Moreover, the company could maintain a high EPS growth rate of 30-40% in years beyond 2030. This dynamic illustrates that S1 likely has a very long and sustainable sales and earnings runway. In addition, S1 has shown a tenacity for surpassing consensus expectations and could earn EPS toward the higher end of the analysts' estimate ranges. For instance, in a slightly more bullish case scenario, S1 could deliver about $0.50 in EPS next year and possibly around $0.80 in 2027 (fiscal 2028).

This dynamic suggests that S1 may be trading at only about 20-30 forward earnings estimates, which is cheap. Furthermore, if we consider its very robust 50% (roughly) annual EPS growth rate, S1's forward price-to-earnings growth "PEG" ratio may only be about 0.5 here.

This valuation is extremely cheap for a high-quality growth tech stock, as companies in this space could have a PEG ratio of about 1.5 and still be considered relatively inexpensive, in my view.

Where SentinelOne Could Be In The Future

Year (fiscal) 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Sales (in billions) $1.01 $1.26 $1.55 $1.90 $2.30
Sales growth 23% 25% 23% 22% 22%
EPS $0.25 $0.50 $0.80 $1.20 $1.55
EPS growth 400% 100% 60% 50% 29%
Forward P/E ratio

50

 47 45 42 40
Stock price $25 $38 $55 $65 $80

Source: The Financial Prophet

I'm implementing a relatively modest growth rate and multiples, and I believe this to be about the base case scenario for S1. However, we could see higher than anticipated growth, a higher multiple, and a higher stock price sooner in a more bullish case outcome.

Tariffs and other transitory uncertainties could be holding back some growth and profitability prospects at S1. SentinelOne could perform much better as the macroeconomic landscape improves and more certainty returns to markets in 2026 and beyond.

SentinelOne remains my top cybersecurity holding, and I have a $28 12-month price target on the stock.

Risks to SentinelOne

S1 faces numerous risks despite my bullish assessment. The number one risk may be the increased competition in the cybersecurity space. While S1 has a solid platform, other companies like CrowdStrike are also well positioned and very active in AI. S1 has to execute extremely well to remain competitive and increase profitability while maintaining solid growth in future quarters and years. There are additional macroeconomic as well as other industry-specific related risks. Investors should examine these and other risks carefully before investing in SentinelOne's shares.

data.title: The Sydney Sweeney Effect: Bullish On American Eagle Post Q2
data.description:
Investment thesis

American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (NYSE:AEO) reported earnings yesterday after the bell, and the stock is soaring. It's up about 27% pre-market, up to the mid-$17 range. Now, of course, the big news this quarter was the Sydney Sweeney campaign ad. They chose their most popular product line in denim for the ad, which boosted sales and attracted new customers from every U.S. county.

The key to this stock going into H2'25 is whether American Eagle can repeat this kind of successful ad campaign next quarter. What I mean by this is that Sydney Sweeney's partnership with American Eagle is set to run until the end of 2025. And management on the earnings call repeatedly hinted at a second ad campaign coming in the fall. With these campaigns, timing is everything. The first ad was actually released just 10 days before Q2 earnings, meaning all the traction and increased sales are set to show up in bulk in Q3. I expect management to repeat the same ad campaign 10 days before Q3, but this time to focus the campaign on their activewear and loungewear product lines.

Now that the stock is up 27% already pre-market, I’m not advising investors to jump into it immediately; a price pullback is likely to follow this rally. However, between now and the end of the year, we should see some significant catalysts for American Eagle stock from a new ad campaign, tied in with some nice EPS tailwinds. Hence, I'm initiating with a buy.

Q2'25 boost

It was stellar earnings for American Eagle. Revenue hit $1.283 billion, down just 1% year over year but still above expectations. Gross profit came in at $500 million with a 38.9% margin, up about 30 base points since last year. In terms of operating income, the company hit $103 million, up 2% since a year ago, with margins expanding to 8%. Diluted EPS hit $0.45, up 15% year over year.

On the balance sheet, things looked fairly good, too; inventory costs are at $718 million. Yes, inventory is up 8% since Q2 '24, but the increase is mainly due to tariffs. The company holds about $192 million in cash and cash equivalents. The big news this earnings is that they managed to get debt down to zero, compared to the $203 million debt they held last earnings. It was also a good quarter in terms of buybacks, with the company purchasing $200 million in an accelerated share repurchase; about 18 million shares were acquired. It brings the year-to-date purchases total up to $231 million, roughly 10% of diluted shares. $21 million was paid in dividends for the quarter, giving each shareholder about $0.125 per share.

As for guidance, the company is expecting about $255 to $265 million in operating income for the full year 2025 but is expecting a slight dip in gross margins going into those tariff headwinds. Again, this quarter, their Aerie segment continues to outperform and remains the main growth driver. We’ve seen a lot of buybacks this year; the total is now over 20 million shares. This, along with their debt elimination, provides some nice EPS tailwinds going into H2'25.

A big push from Aerie

American Eagle’s revenue mix tells me a clear story. If we look at the numbers, their core American Eagle brand seems to be under a bit of pressure. However, Aerie is picking up the weight, becoming the company’s growth engine.

Let's take a look at the chart I made below. This quarter, American Eagle revenue fell roughly 3% year-over-year to $800 million. We’re seeing a bit of weaker demand and some softer sales in this segment. Even on a year-to-date basis, the brand is down roughly 4%. Now, if we look at the flip side, their Aerie segment posted a 3% revenue gain this quarter to $429 million.

This growth is what’s helping offset American Eagle’s decline. To me, this shows its importance to the company’s top line moving forward. The growth from Aerie is showing a stronger sell-through and healthier full-price sales. But most importantly, it's showing great success at bringing back the younger demographics, as we will discuss. Although it's now much smaller, Aerie contributes roughly one-third of quarterly revenue and is expanding faster than the AE brand. Moving forward, I expect American Eagle’s performance to depend increasingly on Aerie’s momentum.

Another ad campaign coming?

Let's go back to the launch date for the Sydney Sweeney ad campaign. On July 23rd, the ad debuted, featuring Sydney Sweeney in a denim-centric spot titled “Sydney Sweeney Has Great Jeans.” As part of the same rollout, the company introduced the "Sydney Jean," a limited variety of their Dreamy Drape Jeans. This ad, of course, got a massive amount of attention and reach. It was described by Craig Brommers, the CMO, as the most successful campaign to date, shattering the previous records in new customer sales. It generated a whopping 40 billion in new impressions. The Sydney Jean soon sold out in its first week, and her jacket in just one day. Brommers touted the campaign's success heavily on the earnings call. He mentioned the following:

We’ve experienced denim sellouts of items that Sydney has worn. We have strong positive traffic throughout this quarter, a staggering 40 billion impressions.

He also hinted at the longevity of this campaign, saying:

But a brand campaign is not to be judged in just 1 day, 1 week, or even 1 month, a brand campaign endures. We are off to a start beyond our wildest dreams.

To me, that signals more to come from American Eagle and, most importantly, from Sydney Sweeney.

In terms of stock movement, the initial ad helped boost the stock about 21% within days, with Q2 earnings pushing the rally higher. Overall, since the ad campaign in July, the stock is up about 34%. So it's clear to me that American Eagle's stock and revenue heavily depend on the face of Sydney Sweeney. Brommers even mentioned on the call that:

Sydney will be part of our team as we get into the back half of the year, and we'll be introducing new elements of the campaign as we continue forward.

So the good news is that Sydney Sweeney will remain a central figure for American Eagle through the rest of the year. We should see more of her presence beyond just the initial ad campaign. However, while they confirmed her involvement through year-end, there are no specific follow-up campaign details yet. But with the success of the first campaign, I find it extremely unlikely that management won't pull out all the stops to get her back for another ad. I believe the denim campaign is complete, and the company has sufficient ad material on denim for the rest of 2025.

We could see a second Sydney Sweeney ad concentrated around their activewear and loungewear product line. This will also include their intimates line, encompassing a range of items such as underwear, pajamas, loungewear, and swimwear. I see this as the main catalyst for the stock in the near term. Aerie’s activewear and intimates are logical fits if American Eagle wants to maintain its current sales momentum, especially with Aerie driving strong growth right now and resonating with Sweeney's much younger fanbase. It would also be a great ad pick going into the fall. The timing of this upcoming ad campaign will be critical, as I will explain.

Timing is everything

Moving into the fall, a second ad campaign will be pivotal; their creative director is signaling a seasonal campaign, stating:

There's more to come. We're thinking about some exciting holiday campaigns and how we can leverage the product within these campaigns.

So we now know for sure we should see a second ad released between now and the end of the year. There's some concern about all the positives being priced into the stock after the initial ad and the better-than-expected quarter. I'm not too worried, considering that management explicitly noted continued momentum in August and into the next quarter. The CFO actually pointed out that Q3 comps had already turned positive by late August, partly on the back of the ad campaigns. The guided Q3 comps are up low single digits, and that operating income of $95 to $100 million, explicitly including tariff headwinds, tells me they were confident the marketing lift would outweigh margin pressure.

Another advantage of the timing was dropping the ad right before the back-to-school rush. It's traditionally been one of their best-selling time frames, where we usually see some heavy shopping weeks and denim spikes.

Valuation

As per Seeking Alpha, American Eagle looks pretty attractive, despite the recent share spike. The stock is currently trading at a forward price-to-sales ratio of just 0.46, which is more than 50% below the sector median of 0.96, and a forward EV-to-sales ratio of 0.78, roughly 40% below the sector median of 1.31. These are some pretty significant discounts. They tell me that the market is underappreciating American Eagle's cash generation next quarter. Looking at profitability multiples, forward EV-to-EBITDA is 10.3; even this is still a 5% discount to the sector median of 10.89. Trailing EV-to-EBITDA is only 7.8, nearly 32% below peers. Layered on top of this is a powerful brand momentum story. We have to take these numbers alongside the fact that the campaign has already driven 700,000 new customers into stores. Denim sellouts are validating that real demand right now.

Remember, this stock still hasn't recovered its pre-Liberation Day levels, down 20% since the year started. So even though we should expect a cool-down following this post-earnings spike, I think investors should look for solid entry points between now and Q3.

What's Next?

Going into H2'25, we should see a second ad campaign launch. Following that campaign, we should see similar positive stock movement swings. I'm expecting the timing of this second campaign to fall in mid-to-late October, with the branding focusing primarily on their activewear/loungewear product line.

data.title: AudioEye: Poised For Growth, Priced For Reality
data.description:
AudioEye, Inc. (NASDAQ:AEYE), in my opinion, despite acting for a good cause, already appears to be correctly priced, with low downside or upside potential. It is currently a hold, even if it's presenting a moderate growth in its segments.

The Company

Founded in 2005, it operates on a SaaS subscription model as a provider of digital accessibility solutions for companies, with a tool that offers services to find accessibility issues, helping these companies to follow compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act [ADA] and Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) standards in the United States.

It became public in the year 2013 through a reverse merger, acquiring control of Cumberland Systems, with its ticker eventually being changed to AEYE, in which AudioEye's management took command of the new public company. At the moment, the company is the only publicly traded digital accessibility company listed on Nasdaq.

AudioEye business serves a diversified base of over 127,000 customers, based on SaaS subscriptions aiming to generate recurring and predictable revenues. Its solutions offer AI automations that promise to fix up to 50% of detected problems, performing scans and detecting common digital accessibility errors, and also recognizing that this artificial intelligence does not have the capacity to solve all problems by itself, and for this reason, it complement it with a team that performs usability tests and implements direct code fixes. The company reports that this approach detects 500% more problems than fully automated tools, this being a potential for growth and a vulnerability, because despite having a more legally defensible offer, the human services can exert pressure on its gross margins as the company grows.

Q2 Results

In the last quarter, the company presented its 38th consecutive quarter of revenue growth, reaching the level of $9.9 million, a 16% increase compared to the previous year, while its final net income was negative $0.002 million, almost breaking even. However, the main reason for this less negative result is an accounting change of the stock warrants that the company has, which represented a value of $1.36 million. Not coming from operations and also not representing a cash inflow, only a reduction of the liability (which varies according to the stock price).

Without such a benefit, the company's net income would be negative $1.36 million, a drop of 85% in relation to the same quarter of the previous year.

This is due to two main reasons: a significant and disproportionate increase in selling and marketing expenses to aggressively increase its revenue and an increase in administrative expenses, despite the adverse situation the company has been going through, with its stock price falling 43% in the last 12 months.

Sector

The company operates within the digital accessibility market, a subsector of the software sector that has been gaining visibility due to regulatory factors, which have been applying stronger laws regarding digital accessibility.

The digital accessibility software market has variable growth, as it is still in a phase of maturation and definition. Estimates for the market size in the year 2023 vary between $721.1 million by Grand View Research, and $754 million by Market.us, and for the future, even if the projections are divergent, they all point to a growth from $800 million to more than $1.1 billion, with growth rates between 6% and 10% annually. Despite this, there is still a consensus about the market, which believes that the software segment for website accessibility is the fastest growing, where companies are the ones that invest the most, thus representing 62% of total spending, with North America holding the largest regional market, with 42% of participation.

This growth is being driven by catalysts that transform the adoption of accessibility technologies into an operational necessity and not just an option, and as a result, legislation and regulation are the main growth drivers, where laws like the ADA and EAA develop projects for digital accessibility and inclusion, forcing companies to invest in these solutions. In addition to regulatory issues, there is an increase in awareness about the importance of online accessibility.

AudioEye's positioning is a way to differentiate itself by its business model, also considering that it is the only company in the sector that is publicly listed on Nasdaq, offering it access to public capital. However, the digital accessibility market is described as highly fragmented, not having a company with a dominant share.

The regulatory issues, despite functioning as catalysts creating demand while validating the market, can also attract greater attention to the sector, potentially intensifying competition, considering that there are already better-capitalized private companies in operation, such as Level Access, and others that have the support of private equity investors, like KKR and JMI. Due to the lack of publicly traded digital accessibility companies, a direct comparison becomes impossible.

Valuation

Revenue

AEYE divides its revenue and operations into two main segments, corresponding to its two sales channels:

Enterprise Channel: This segment serves large companies with a strong digital presence, and as they are larger, they involve a longer contract cycle, in which AEYE offers its complete platform and other special services. The revenue comes from subscription contracts, being a higher value and lower volume annual recurring revenue; this segment represents 45% of its total revenue.

Partner and Marketplace Channel: This segment serves small to medium companies, and they integrate the AEYE's solution into their services and resell it to their client bases. This revenue comes from a large volume of subscriptions with a lower individual value, showing a recent revenue increase of 17% in relation to the previous period. And it represents the greatest upside potential, given that it is easier to grow on smaller bases, especially in an environment of falling interest rates.

For Partner and Marketplace, management projects a 17% growth in the segment, totaling a plausible $23.7 million in line with the average growth of 14% since 2021. This segment will be driven especially by the European Accessibility Act (EAA), which requires any non-microenterprise in Europe to become accessible, opening a window of opportunity for companies that are at the adoption deadline. And a lower growth of 10% for the enterprises segment, in line with the deceleration it presented in recent years, presenting a total of $16.4 million given the greater difficulty in capturing large clients, especially with the productivity gain of programmers with the advances of AI.

P/S

Historically, its P/S multiple has oscillated a lot along with the company's performance and market expectation, especially the expectation of it carrying out a more defined cost containment and continuing the past growth it presented. Only in the last 12 months, it reached a P/S of 11.91 in November of last year, down to the current 3.95 found today.

Assuming a stabilization of the P/S around 3.9x, as it has been trading since April of this year, and a projected revenue of $40.1 million, with a share dilution of 3% in line with recent years, we obtain the final price per share of $12.21.

Where, by performing a sensitivity analysis with a standard deviation of 0.3x on its multiple, and of 1.5% on its total revenue, we can obtain the value range of $10.9 to $13.5, representing that in the worst-case scenario, there would be a downside of 9.7%, and in the best-case scenario, an upside potential of 11.4%.

Gross Profit

The company's gross margin is historically high, being a common characteristic in software companies, being situated between 75% and 79%. For having this already healthy gross margin, I believe the upside from the use of AI is relatively low. Given that most of the company's expenses are in SG&A, things are harder to replace than bug fixes in the gross margin.

However, the company has doubled its debt since Q4 of 2024 to a total of $12.8 million, to continue financing the revenue growth necessary to hopefully perpetuate customers. Finding itself, in my opinion, in a situation of being hostage to its high proportional costs. However, in the last quarter, the company pointed to cost reduction measures, reducing SG&A to values 20% lower than the previous quarter, stabilizing its R&D at $1.2 million. Where, if the company manages to gain more scale, and perpetuate its customers through accessible contracts, it will eventually become profitable.

Drivers and Risks

In the short term, in my point of view, AudioEye has several short- and medium-term catalysts, such as the impact of the European Accessibility Act (EAA), because with its entry into force in June of this year, it creates a unified and mandatory digital accessibility market throughout the European region, a regional market that the company is beginning to enter, being of a comparable scale to the United States. Management has been capitalizing on this demand, as it demonstrated that its sales in Europe tripled at the beginning of the third quarter, and as it captures this demand, even if a small percentage, it has the potential to significantly accelerate its revenue growth for the next year. If it manages to get a larger percentage of this demand in the European market, it will prove that it cannot only identify a global catalyst but can also make a profit on it, leading to a re-evaluation, reducing its risks.

Another short-term opportunity also refers to a new rule from the Department of Justice under Title II of the ADA, because by establishing WCAG 2.1 AA (a set of specific technical rules to guarantee accessibility on sites and apps) as a technical standard for all state and local government entities, with deadlines for 2026 to 2027, it creates predictable demand, managing direct sales to the public sector.

The most relevant and imminent risk at this moment is the intensification of competition, as the company already faces high competition, with already matured companies in the sector like Level Access, which has a larger scale with greater availability of resources. This pressure can limit AEYE's pricing power, further compressing its margins and making it difficult to conquer parts of the market and to be successful in capturing the increasing demands.

Conclusion

With a target price of $12.21, the low upside potential and the fact that the company is already fairly priced support my hold recommendation, even if its revenue and segments are showing to grow rapidly.

data.title: Beacon Financial May Require More Time To Build For The Future
data.description:
In early May, I wrote my first analysis of Beacon Financial Corporation (NYSE: BBT). At the time, the company was called Berkshire Hills Bancorp and traded under the ticker symbol BHLB. I rated the stock as a Buy, as the company had recently reported strong earnings that had exceeded analysts' expectations. Berkshire Hills had also revealed that only 0.42% of its loans were delinquent or non-performing which was the lowest such rate for the bank in almost two decades. I stated that a pullback in BBT’s price from February and March had given investors an attractive entry point for new investments in the company.

Fast forward to the start of September, and my optimism for the company has been put on hold. The bank’s stock has been trading in a tight range since May with a total return of only 5% compared to the S&P 500’s gains of 13.4%. However, Berkshire Hills Bancorp officially completed a merger with Brookline Bancorp (BRKL) on September 1. This “merger of equals” is what prompted the change in the company's name and ticker.

Berkshire Hills made its last quarterly report in July which showed many of the same strengths that I noted from the first quarter of the year. I believe the relatively stagnant behavior of its stock was caused, in part, by some investors waiting on the sidelines until the merger was complete. However, now that the company has completed the addition of the assets from Brookline Bancorp, I have taken a more careful look at what it has acquired. I am less enthusiastic about this new company even though I think that it still has a great deal of potential. I am changing the previous Buy rating for BHLB and assigning a Hold to BBT.

Company Overview

Post merger, Beacon Financial Corporation controls almost $24 billion in assets, easily placing the company as one of the 100 largest banks in the US. Assuming no real growth from the others on the list, BBT would have been around 79th in the most recent rankings based on controlled assets at the end of Q2 2025. The company operates over 145 branches throughout New England from its headquarters in Boston, MA. Beacon Financial currently operates under the bank names of Berkshire Bank, Brookline Bank, BankRI and PCSB Bank. It also has other smaller subsidiaries including an SBA lending division and a private wealth services division that controls over $3 billion in assets under management.

Below is a chart showing the financial data reported by both Berkshire Hills and Brookline at the end of Q2 2025. Brookline’s operations show a higher tolerance for risk as its loans outstanding are higher than its deposit base. Its higher ratio of net loans to total assets also illustrates its tendency to reach for greater income while taking on a potentially higher rate of loan defaults. Both banks have a net loans to total assets ratio that is above the Q2 2025 average for banks of its size, according to the FDIC.

Berkshire Bancorp

Brookline Bancorp

Total Assets

$12.0 Billion

$11.6 Billion

Total Deposits

$9.98 Billion

$8.96 Billion

Total Loans Outstanding

$9.50 Billion

$9.58 Billion

Net Loans/ Total Assets

78.2%

81.7%

Prior to the closing of the merger, Brookline stated that the combination of Berkshire Hills and itself would create a top 10 footprint for deposits in 14 of the 19 largest metropolitan areas in the US. Estimates for projected cost savings by the two companies are in the range of $65 to $70 million annually. This should be achieved by reducing loan concentration risks and taking advantage of operational synergies. Below is a map of the current locations for the branches that were acquired from Brookline.

The stock for Beacon Financial currently yields 2.76% based on the previous payouts from Berkshire Hills. Brookline had a yield of 4.93% prior to September 1. Berkshire Hills had not raised its quarterly dividend since November of 2022, but expectations are that an increase will be forthcoming.

Analysis of Beacon Financial

When Beacon Financial releases its Q3 2025 earnings on October 23, we will have a chance to have a clearer picture of the combined operations of Berkshire Hills and Brookline. Prior to September 1, both banks were trading at reasonable valuations based on their previously reported earnings. Berkshire Hills had a PE of around 10. Brookline was trading at a profit multiple of 9. When the transaction closed, the stock price for Brookline was $0.25 below its last reported tangible book value.

Below is a chart showing other financial metrics for both banks at the end of Q2, their final full quarters as stand along entities. When comparing both companies’ year-over-year profit growth in the first six months of this year, the numbers are good for Berkshire Hills at 24%, but they even better for Brookline at just over 34%. Both are especially strong for companies that were previously trading with PE multiples of 10 or lower.

Berkshire Hills Bancorp

Brookline Bancorp

YoY Earnings Growth (Half Year)

24.0%

34.3%

Return on Assets (Q2)

1.03%

0.77%

Return on Equity (Q2)

9.97%

7.04%

Net Interest Margin (Q2)

3.27%

3.32%

Efficiency Ratio (Q2)

56.73%

61.34%

Yield on Total Loans

6.19%

6.01%

Cost of Interest-Bearing Deposits

2.80%

2.92%

% CRE as a % of Total Assets

28.9%

47.4%

In most other categories, Berkshire Hills appears to have an advantage with a higher return on assets and return on equity. Its efficiency ratio is lower by 461 basis points. It also has a higher earning loan portfolio and a lower cost of interest-bearing deposits. However, the loan yields are below the national average for banks of their size. The deposit costs are higher than that same standard. Based on these figures, I would not rate Brookline Bancorp as a Buy as a stand along company. Therefore, I was challenged to decide if Berkshire Hills would be able to improve the conditions for its new partner, or if Brookline would be an anchor that pulled down the current version of Berkshire Hills.

In my first article, I noted the apparently diversity of the potential new loan portfolio that could be created by the combination of the two banks. However, after deeper review, I am increasingly concerned about the sizeable amount of commercial real estate loans being brought over from Brookline. Its percent of commercial real estate when compared to its total assets at the end of Q2 was 47.4%. The national average for a bank with $10 billion to $250 billion in assets is 18.3%. For banks with under $10 billion, it is still significantly lower than Brookline's 47.4%, with an average of 34.3%.

Below is the most recent report of the loans from Brookline Bancorp with the heavy CRE concentration evident in the top line. As part of its Q2 report, Brookline stated that it increased its allowance for loan losses coverage to 1.32%, indicating a slight elevation in concerns for the risk that this portfolio holds. Brookline also announced its ratio of commercial real estate to total risk based capital was 363%, which is above the threshold from the FDIC to signal the potential need for higher risk management practices.

Additional Risks to Consider

As with any merger, there is risk that the union of Berkshire Hills and Brookline will not be as beneficial as planned. There could be challenges integrating the operating systems and corporate cultures of the two companies. Cost savings may be less than expected, and the new bank may struggle to retain its customers in a very competitive market in Boston, New York, and other regions in the Northeast.

Even though Beacon Financial now has a larger footprint, the bank's geographic diversity is still limited to just a few states, which makes its operations more sensitive to regional employment trends, political instability, and potential declines in residential property values. Berkshire Hills and Brookline both had a high concentration of their activity in the Boston area and around the state of Massachusetts. There are several recent events that could impact the growth potential for BBT. For example, an article that appeared in June in Axios detailed how cuts in research funding at Harvard could cause a decline in Metro Boston’s GDP and affect as many as 15,500 jobs. Another article from a professor at Boston University highlighted cuts that will impact Moderna (MRNA), which has its headquarters in the Boston area, as well as declines in research spending at MIT and the University of Massachusetts.

The Bottom Line

When I wrote my first analysis of Berkshire Hills, now Beacon Financial, I did so largely from the perspective of Berkshire Hills. I have had more time now to study the other half of this merger, and my enthusiasm for the new company, at least in the short-term, has waned. I think the results from Q3 could be somewhat choppy as the new company aligns the operations from Brookline with its higher standards. To use a construction metaphor, there appear to be a few cracks in the foundation that need to be shored up before a better structure can be built on top.

I would not recommend adding to any positions of BBT at this time, but I will continue to follow the company to see how it performs in the future to see if it can leverage the new larger scale of its operations to its advantage.

data.title: 4 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Discover the power of dividend stocks, your ticket to consistent and growing income. What a wonderful feeling it is to sit back and collect regular paychecks from everyday companies just by holding their shares. Embrace a strategy that helps your returns keep rising and your financial future look brighter. Now is a great time to invest in dividend stocks and reap the rewards they can offer.

Companies that regularly raise their dividends tend to be great long-term investments. Dividend increases suggest financial strength and stability, indicating that a company can generate reliable cash flow. It also requires deliberate capital allocation planning by management to make it a core tenet of the business. Consistent dividend growth typically indicates that a company is generating reliable cash flow, which in turn enables it to offer investors a combination of income and capital appreciation.

From the broader universe of investable stocks, I focus on those with a proven track record of annual dividend increases. I watch dividend increase announcements and combine those with other supporting data points. This information is core to my investment process, and I share it here with the Seeking Alpha community to help you make more informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll spotlight another group of companies and provide more detail on both the selections and the methodology behind them.

A few highlights from this week's list:

  • Average dividend increase: 6.6% (median: 6.7%)

  • Average dividend streak: 18 years (median: 15 years)

  • This week consists of all financial stocks!

My strategy focuses on buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that raise their dividends annually and have the potential to outperform relevant benchmarks over time.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining multiple data sources: the "U.S. Dividend Champions" spreadsheet provides the universe of companies I review, and then upcoming dividend announcements. This combination joins data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company on this list has a minimum of 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories that I'll use throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10-24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 1
Contender 2
Challenger 1

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Capital City Bank Group (CCBG) 12 2.41 8-Sep-25 8.33% Contender
Broadridge Financial Solutions, Inc. (BR) 18 1.57 11-Sep-25 10.80% Contender
Community Financial System, Inc. (CBU) 33 3.17 12-Sep-25 2.17% Champion
Silvercrest Asset Management Group Inc. (SAMG) 8 5.25 12-Sep-25 5.00% Challenger

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date by which you must own the stock to receive the dividend.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
CCBG 0.24 0.26 8.33%
BR 0.88 0.98 10.80%
CBU 0.46 0.47 2.17%
SAMG 0.2 0.21 5.00%

Additional Metrics

Here are additional metrics related to these companies. Some data points include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
CCBG 43.22 32.38 44.04 13.05 33% Off Low 2% Off High
BR 249.84 206.95 271.91 36.19 21% Off Low 8% Off High
CBU 59.35 49.44 73.39 21.24 20% Off Low 19% Off High
SAMG 15.99 13.54 19.2 11.47 18% Off Low 17% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the "Chowder Rule," the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
SAMG 5.25 5.3 5.6 5.2 5.3 10.3
CBU 3.17 2.2 2.3 2.3 4.4 5.6
CCBG 2.41 14.6 13.7 11.7 23.9 14.1
BR 1.57 10 11.2 10.3 12.6 11.8

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD features strong historical performance, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $69B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend-growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four key factors that SCHD tracks in its index.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

YCharts was having issues this week, so I went back to my old stock return calculator.

For reference, SCHD returned about 220%, or 12.3% per year. The top dog of the group was Broadridge Financial, with a 480% total return (19.2% CAGR).

CCBG was the only other one that managed to outperform SCHD with a 253% total return (13.4% annualized).

The last two in the group were CBU at 124% and SAMG at 111%. Unfortunately, these last two significantly underperformed SCHD.

Next Steps

I'm selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. In my individual sleeve portion of my portfolio, I have 33 holdings. So, the bar is pretty high due to diminishing returns of adding an additional holding. I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD and stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what's performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

Last week, I decided to add Unity to my watchlist as I learn more about the company. This week, I want to take another look at Broadridge Financial. I've looked at them before, and I'm intrigued with their historical performance. But I want to make sure forward-looking performance seems strong as well.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Northwest Natural Has Value, Growth, And Yield
data.description:
Successful investing doesn’t have to be a popularity contest. In fact, some of my best picks are ones off the beaten path and can be found in both bull and bear markets alike. That’s because it’s a market for stocks rather than the stock market, and there is always something on sale due to market irrationality. Sooner or later, the market pendulum will swing back toward quality dividend stocks that are undervalued, and patient investors will reap the reward while getting paid a solid income stream in the meantime.

This brings me to Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN), which I last covered in June, noting its mix of value and income and strategic expansion. What’s great for value investors is that NWN has been rangebound, and currently trades just 0.44% higher than when I last covered it, offering opportunities to layer into the stock. As shown below, NWN currently trades in the middle of its 52-week range while sporting a 4.7% yield.

In this article, I revisit NWN including recent business fundamentals, and discuss what makes it an appealing value and income stock in this frothy market, so let’s get started!

Why NWN?

Northwest Natural Holding Company is a diversified utility based in Portland Oregon. It has roots dating back to the 1800s and has 69 consecutive years of dividend payments. NWN’s business is broken into Natural Gas Utility, Water, Renewables, and most recently SiEnergy, a high-growth utility in Texas. Notably, NWN’s Texas footprint was expanded further this year with the acquisition of Hughes Gas Resources, which was rebranded as Pines Holdings.

NWN continued to deliver strong results during Q2 2025. This included gas utility margin rising by $17 million due to a new rate implemented in November of last year in Oregon with a 9.5% allowed ROE. Segment net income rose by $4.5 million, partially offset by higher O&M (operating & maintenance) and depreciation. At the same time, NWN’s water utilities added $1.8 million (or $0.04 per share) in net income due to new rates in Arizona, contributions from acquired utilities, and customer growth of 6%.

Renewables continues to provide steady income, as its RNG (renewable natural gas) platform is backed by long-term, fixed-price contracts with investment grade rated counterparties. Importantly, SiEnergy contributed $1 million in net income (or $0.03 per share) during the second quarter. This was driven by both organic growth and the Pines acquisition, which added to the backlog and number of customers. Pines alone added 7,000 connections northeast of Houston and has a robust backlog of 12,000 meters. This contributed to robust 10.6% YoY customer growth for all of NWN’s utilities.

Management is guiding for 5% EPS growth annually at the midpoint of the range over the medium term. NWN is also expected to spend $450 to $500 million in capital expenditures this year on grid modernization, meter replacements, and storage reinforcement. Over the medium term, NWN also benefits from population inflows into Texas, which gives its gas utility there multi-year growth visibility. Plus, NWN’s water utilities offer consolidation potential across the sector as communities increasingly rely on private operators to fund infrastructure upgrades.

NWN carries a strong balance sheet with an A- credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a safe FFO to debt target of 14% and $550 million of liquidity, including $103 million of cash on hand. Importantly for income investors, NWN carries a respectable 4.7% dividend yield that’s well-covered by a 65% payout ratio, leaving plenty of retained capital to fund growth.

Lastly, I continue to see value in NWN at the current price of $41.35 with a forward PE of 14.2, sitting far below its historical PE of 19.8, as shown below.

Sell side analysts who follow the company estimate 5-7% annual EPS growth over the next two years. I believe this is reasonable due to organic customer growth, base rate increases, and territory expansion, including its backlog in Texas. This, combined with the near-5% dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, could result in market-beating total returns from here.

Risks to NWN include the threat of wildfires due to a leak or rupture in gas pipelines, especially when it comes to forested areas of Oregon and Washington. Cheaper electricity due to low-cost solar could compete with natural gas for home heating. Improved customer energy efficiency, such as heat pumps, could reduce natural gas demand.

Investor Takeaway

Northwest Natural Holding stands out as a compelling value and income play in a volatile market. It has a long dividend growth track record and a well-covered 4.7% yield. This is backed by regulated utility operations and expansion into high-growth areas like Texas through SiEnergy and Pines Holdings. NWN’s recent performance, including rate-driven margin gains and strong customer growth across all segments, supports potential 5-7% EPS growth annually. Lastly, trading at a forward PE well below its historical average, NWN offers patient investors an attractive entry point with potential for market-beating total returns from here.

data.title: What Will Light A Fire Under Small Caps?
data.description:
By Jeff Weniger

The house of pain continues with small caps, at least on a relative basis. Year-to-date, the S&P 600 index has posted a 3.0% gain, so it's not like money is being burned. But still, even the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond index is up 4.9% YTD and the S&P 500 is up 10.9%.1

BofA's fund flow data tells a thousand words.

Figure 1: A Tale of Two Asset Classes

We're at a moment of truth with large versus small, too. When I plot the S&P 100 relative to the big, broad Wilshire 5000 index, it is making its third attempt at resistance levels that date to the 1990s. If recent action holds, this would mark an important breakout for large caps.

Figure 2: A Moment of Truth

Because of small caps' multi-year struggle, the group is trading at a 21.8% discount to large caps on reported earnings (figure 3). It doesn't mean a small-cap resurgence will start tomorrow, but I do find solace in these valuation gaps resembling the situation that manifested in both the mid-1970s and the turn of the century.

Figure 3: Small Caps' Relative Valuations Are Promising

Small caps are cheap, at least relatively, but there is a potential 2026 headwind that we should address.

The S&P 600 index of small caps has an easy hurdle this calendar year, in that the Street is penciling in earnings growth of just 2.7% to the S&P 500's 7.4%, according to Yardeni Research. But for next year, the Street has S&P 600 earnings jumping another 18.8% (the S&P 500 is expected to see earnings growth of 13.9%). Those will probably both be revised down, as usual, but it's still an easier hurdle for large caps than small caps.

As for the Dollar

It's one of the most common questions we get on large versus small. I wish I had some strong view here, but I don't. The problem with the dollar is you must get two things correct to make money:

  • First, you have to forecast where the dollar is going, which is as good as heads versus tails
  • Then, you must accurately identify what will happen to small and large caps if you get the dollar right

It's easier said than done, as I think figure 4 makes clear. Even if you accurately predict the dollar, good luck making heads or tails out of large and small because of it.

Figure 4: Predicting This Stuff Isn't Easy

I have given the "dollar vs. cap size" concept a ton of thought over the years.

The common refrain goes something like this: "If the dollar rises, that is going to be bad for big U.S. multinationals, because their costs will be in expensive dollars and their customers will pay them in weak euros and weak yen. Therefore, if I'm bullish the dollar, I should go for small caps."

If it were only that simple. Dollar up, give me small caps. Everyone knows that. But it isn't necessarily true.

How about if "dollar up" is happening because some hedge fund is blowing up or a banking institution is dying in real time? In those markets, people are doing wild things like getting diamonds into Swiss safe deposit boxes, emptying ATMs and so on. In that world, where the grab for dollars is on, you probably aren't going to be happy with small caps.

Moral of the story: like small caps because you like small caps, not because of any U.S. dollar forecast that is on your mind.

Now…that doesn't mean you can't engage in some old-fashioned Fed Kremlinology. That work could help small caps because of a narrative that figure 5 lays out. It shows a concept that is intuitive: as a collective, large caps tend to stand on sturdier balance sheet ground than small caps. Interest coverage (earnings before interest and taxes divided by interest expense) is a notably different scene as you move across the cap spectrum.

Figure 5: Interest Coverage Ratio

If the Fed starts cutting rates, that helps not only people and companies with large debt burdens, but also those who are more likely to have floating-rate obligations. Both of those descriptors apply more to small caps than large caps. It's something to consider if the Fed starts easing policy.

Figure 6 sorts our four U.S. small-cap Funds by quality (return on equity).

Figure 6: U.S. Small-Cap Fundamentals

The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the respective ticker: DES, EES, DGRS, QSML.

Funds mentioned in figure 6:

  • DES: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend Fund
  • EES: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Fund
  • DGRS: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund
  • QSML: WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Growth Fund

1 Performance figures from WisdomTree's PATH software, as of 8/27/25.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or smaller companies increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Jeff Weniger, CFA, Head of Equity Strategy

Jeff Weniger, CFA, is Head of Equity Strategy at WisdomTree, where he helps shape the firm’s stock market outlook by blending macroeconomic insights with fundamental analysis. With over a decade of experience in investment strategy, Jeff previously served as Director and Senior Strategist at BMO in the office of the CIO, contributing to the Asset Allocation Committee and co-managing ETF model portfolios across the U.S. and Canada. Notably, in 2013, at age 32, Jeff became the youngest member selected for BMO’s Global Investment Forum, joining the firm’s top strategists to craft its long-term investment outlook.

Jeff holds a B.S. in Finance from the University of Florida and an MBA from Notre Dame, and has been a CFA charterholder since 2006. A recognized voice in financial media, he frequently appears on CNBC Asia, Canada’s Business News Network (BNN), and Wharton Business Radio, and contributes insights to leading outlets including Barron’s and the Wall Street Journal. Jeff also has an extensive social media following, engaging a broad audience with thoughtful insight and market perspectives.

Original Post

data.title: Oak Valley Bancorp: A Loan Book With Zero Loans Past Due
data.description:
Introduction

Oak Valley Bancorp (NASDAQ:OVLY) is the holding company for the Oak Valley Community Bank, which started operations in the early nineties. The bank currently has 18 branches, with a 19th anticipated to open in Lodi, in the San Francisco hinterland.

Seeing a bank with consistently zero loan loss provisions is pretty unique

The main reason why I’m interested in this local bank is the fact its loan loss provisions are consistently zero, which is unique in the sector. Granted, there are always banks that were able to recover previously record provisions but it's very rare to see a bank not having to record any provisions at all.

That’s the case at Oak Valley Bancorp. As you can see below on the income statement, the bank reported interest income of $21.4M, which is an increase of approximately $1.2M compared to the second quarter of 2024. And although the total interest expenses also increased, the net interest income increased by approximately 5% to $18.15M. And as the income statement below shows, the total amount of loan loss provisions was once again zero.

The bank did have to deal with higher non-interest expenses while the amount of non-interest income slightly decreased. This still resulted in a pre-tax income of $7.2M, but this is a decrease of approximately $0.25M compared to the same quarter a year before. The bottom line shows a net profit of just under $5.6M resulting in an EPS of $0.68 (and $0.67 per diluted share). This pushed the H1 2025 EPS to $1.32. The main pressure on the bank’s earnings is coming from the "other expenses" and an increase in the G&A expenses, which Oak Valley blames on the general operating costs to grow the loan book.

Oak Valley Bancorp currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share, which means its payout ratio is currently less than 50%. This allows the bank to retain the majority of its earnings on the balance sheet. This supports the (tangible) book value per share and its plans to pursue loan growth. The bank is planning to open a new branch in Lodi.

A look at the loan book: zero loans are past due

The bank currently has a total of just over $1.9B in assets on its balance sheet, with a very large portion held in liquid and very liquid assets. As you can see below, the bank holds almost $200M in cash and has invested north of $500M in securities available for sale. There are no securities held to maturity which means there's no risk of the fair value being lower than the book value.

Although in excess of 35% of the assets are held in cash and securities, the bank also has a robust loan portfolio of almost $1.1B. As you can see below, the vast majority (in excess of 80%) is classified as commercial real estate, with the majority of that subdivision classified as non-owner occupied.

While there's a general perception commercial real estate loans can be risky, it really depends on the characteristics of the loan as well. A bank can have issued office loans at an 80% LTV ratio, or it may have issued a loan for a commercial space at a 40% LTV ratio. It's thus wrong to consider all CRE loans "dangerous." Oak Valley is the perfect example of this as literally zero of the loans in its loan book are classified as past due. The bank didn’t disclose its LTV ratio in June, but its FY 2024 annual report mentions the average LTV ratio for its CRE portfolio was just 45.3% based on the most recent appraisals (which happened at the time of origination or renewal of the loans).

This explains why no additional loan loss provisions are required, as the bank’s balance sheet already contains an accumulated loan loss provision of in excess of 1% of its loan book.

Investment thesis

I like Oak Valley Bancorp for its conservative loan portfolio. It's very rare to see a bank having to record zero loan loss provisions and not having a single loan past due despite its loan portfolio being heavily concentrated on commercial real estate loans. The low LTV ratio of less than 50% is proving its value when it comes to "asset safety." I will keep an eye on the evolution of the non-interest expenses, but I expect the opening of the new branch in Lodi to be accretive to the bottom line from 2026 on.

At the end of June, the bank’s tangible book value per share was approximately $21.77, which means the stock is trading at a premium of approximately 30% to its tangible book value, but that’s a price I’m rather happy to pay for the loan book quality. Additionally, the 4.2% dividend yield provides a nice compensation while waiting for the next catalyst.

I currently have no position in Oak Valley Bancorp, but this bank is now pretty high up on my list of regional banks I’m keeping an eye on. I may initiate a long position, but this is unlikely to happen in the next 72 hours.

data.title: Roku: Amazon Partnership Vs. Looming Walmart Competition
data.description:
Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) appears to be viewed more for its potential cyclicality rather than its exposure to the online advertising trend. That may be creating a protracted buying opportunity in the stock. The company has no shortage of potential near-term catalysts ranging between margin expansion and a potentially lucrative third-party partnership. There may be looming risks regarding a key channel, but the strong balance sheet and reasonable valuation support optimism. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

ROKU Stock Price

I last covered ROKU in June, when I rated the stock a "Buy" because of the partnership with Amazon (AMZN). The stock has returned around 9% since then.

I see more upside ahead as the company relies on a time-proven playbook.

ROKU Stock Key Metrics

ROKU is known for its smart TV products, allowing consumers to access streaming apps on their televisions. ROKU primarily generates its business through advertisements and commission fees derived from the platform.

In the most recent quarter, ROKU delivered 15% YoY growth in net revenue to $1.111 billion, exceeding guidance of $1.070 billion. Gross profits jumped 17% YoY to $497.7 million, crushing guidance of $465 million. Adjusted EBITDA of $78.2 million also beat guidance of $70 million.

Stock-based compensation still makes up more than adjusted EBITDA, but that ratio has improved greatly. If ROKU can keep up the growth, I expect a serious push into GAAP profitability ahead.

The company ended the quarter with $2.3 billion of cash versus no debt and authorized a $400 million stock repurchase program. Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see $520 million in gross profit, representing 8.3% YoY growth and $110 million in adjusted EBITDA. I suspect the market was disappointed by the growth target, but I note that the profit guide implies solid margin expansion.

On the conference call, management noted that their deal with AMZN was “not exclusive” while also once again downplaying the potential threat from Walmart (WMT) given their acquisition of Vizio. I suspect that investors are also unconvinced regarding management’s lack of concern, especially given that WMT might account for as much as a third of ROKU’s device sales, according to one analyst in 2024. Management did note that they expect the impact from their partnership with AMZN to hit in the fourth quarter at the earliest. Put another way, investors have something to look forward to in terms of a growth catalyst heading into the next year.

Is ROKU Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

As of recent prices, ROKU traded hands at just around 7x gross profits. Consensus estimates call for low-double-digit to high-single-digit top-line growth over the coming years.

The bullish thesis is a bit messy, as benefits from the AMZN partnership might be offset by rising risk from the WMT channel. Based on 30% long-term net margins, the stock trades at roughly 23x long-term earnings. I view this valuation as being distinctly not “dirt cheap” but looking more than reasonable given the net cash balance sheet, positive cash flow generation, as well as the exposure to the secular growth story of online advertising. I suspect that once the company breaks into GAAP profitability, which I estimate to happen within the next four quarters, then the stock will earn serious valuation support and enable it to deliver annual returns in line or in excess of its top-line growth rate.

ROKU Stock Risks

It is possible that ROKU is being disrupted, first by Vizio, then by others. As a consumer, all these streaming apps and platforms feel very interchangeable. It is possible that ROKU over-invests in attempts to overcome that interchangeability. ROKU’s business is sensitive to market health and may face volatility in the event of a market downturn. The stock is not “dirt cheap” and may also face volatility in a selloff. Tariffs may increase the production costs of the product and may hurt cash flow even if investors tend to focus more on the platform's gross profits.

ROKU Stock Conclusion

ROKU still looks like a reasonable bet as a key player in the shift to online advertising. Investors need to look out for potential weakness due to WMT, but I expect the AMZN partnership to deliver a surprise boost come 2026. The company continues to expand profit margins and maintains a net cash balance sheet. ROKU shares many of the traits that I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Metaplanet's M/NAV Hangover And Why The Stack Keeps Climbing
data.description:
Back in early July, we walked through Metaplanet’s (MTPLF) monumental shift from quirky hotels to Asia’s flagship Bitcoin Treasury Company. We talked about the “moving-strike” warrants strategy, zero-coupon bonds, the “555 Million Plan,” and how raising equity at a premium to net asset value [NAV] could keep BTC per share compounding. That was the very flywheel. The somewhat similar flywheel previously embraced by Strategy (MSTR) that Metaplanet now "localised" to Japan. Since then, however, the story has added some grit, as even though the company’s bitcoin pile is meaningfully larger today, the stock is down 45% since my article went out. A pretty disheartening result for a two-month period.

What actually drove the share price slide?

The clearest explanation is persistent m/NAV compression, or a compression in the market multiple paid over Metaplanet’s per-share BTC NAV, which has shrunk significantly.

As you can see from the company's official analytics in the chart, in June and early July, when I published my initial piece, investors were paying several times the NAV to front-run future BTC/share accretion. Through late summer, the premium cooled even as the firm continued to buy bitcoin, hence the multiple compression BTC is trading at the same levels as two months ago.

Meanwhile, the BTC stack didn’t stop

But did Metaplanet really achieve anything meaningful during this period? Yes, because they didn't stop accumulating. Even if a softer multiple made it increasingly less attractive to issue equity to buy BTC, it still made sense to do so, as it is accretive on a BTC/share basis.

Since my July article, when the balance stood at 13,350 BTC, Metaplanet’s buys have been relentless, with today's (Sep.1) disclosure pushing its total holdings to 20,000 BTC.

But more importantly, these BTC buys were quite accretive on a per-share basis, which is the whole foundation of Metaplanet's investment case against, say, buying a BTC ETF. As you can see, Metaplanet now owns ₿0.02111503 per 1,000 shares, up from ₿0.01595568 on July 1st. That's a 32.3% increase in BTC/share in just two months, which also goes to show why it makes sense that the stock trades at a premium, and is likely to continue to do so.

Q2 was also the first quarter where the “Bitcoin Income Generation” line really showed up in the P&L, as management reported ¥1,239 million ($8.4 million) in revenue and ¥817 million ($5.56 million) in operating profit, with ¥1,131 million ($7.7 million) of that revenue coming from cash-secured BTC puts.

The strategy didn’t change; its funding mix did

Now, even though Metaplanet did achieve excellent per-share accretion on its BTC holdings, you can clearly see in the chart that this pace is still nowhere near the pace between the start of the year and June. Of course, the softer multiple explains that. It is still accretive to BTC/share to raise capital close to 2x NAV to buy BTC, but not as much as it is at 6x NAV.

In response to this "problem," just a few days ago, on August 27, Metaplanet’s board approved an international share offering targeting ¥130.3 billion (~$880 million). The company’s notice hints, as we would expect, that most proceeds are earmarked for buying more bitcoin. In the same batch of disclosures, the company temporarily suspended exercises of several moving-strike warrant series, which again is a nod to today’s tighter premium and them seeking more accretive BTC stacking options.

The overseas offering is a big deal because it will broaden the investor base beyond the EVO-driven warrant ecosystem that worked brilliantly when local demand (and m/NAV) was roaring. A marketed international deal should let Metaplanet tap deeper pools of long-only and crossover capital, set a clearing price through bookbuilding, and reduce reliance on the day-to-day warrant exercises to fund BTC purchases, which could re-accelerate treasury growth.

The company also pushed forward a two-year shelf to issue up to ¥555 billion of perpetual preferred shares. In its Q2 presentation, management positions the prefs as BTC-backed credit designed for Japan’s enormous (and, as I explained in my initial article, underutilized) fixed-income market. If approved and launched, that should be a structural way to scale holdings even when the common stock premium wobbles.

Strategy has adopted the same philosophy by building a yield curve across STRF (STRF), STRD (STRD), STRK (STRK), and STRC (STRC), so this could be a similar issuance journey for Metaplanet. In fact, given that Japan is starved of fixed income and income-generating preferred shares, Metaplanet's instruments could prove wildly successful among regional investors.

A quiet but material tailwind: Index inclusion

One more piece that didn’t grab as many headlines is that, last week, FTSE Russell upgraded Metaplanet to mid-cap and added it to the FTSE Japan Index in the September review. This will pull in passive flows that otherwise probably wouldn't have touched a niche Bitcoin treasury name, especially relevant in a market where local, plain-vanilla spot BTC vehicles are limited. This factor, too, should broaden the shareholder base and, potentially, support a higher NAV premium as passive inflows add to the stock's buying volumes.

So… was the drawdown a thesis break or a reset?

If your thesis was “Metaplanet can keep raising at a 6-10x premium forever,” then sure, the past couple of months have been tough. But, if your thesis was “Metaplanet will keep growing its BTC stack and build multiple ways to fund that stack,” the facts line up. The company continues to aggressively stack BTC, increasing the per-share BTC value, even in a challenging market for its equity.

Now it's true that none of this removes the risks. A stubbornly light premium could make the flywheel less accretive for a while, bitcoin’s volatility can swamp any quarter, and the preferred program could go south, for whatever reason, eroding hopes for faster BTC/share accumulation.

But if you buy the core idea that Metaplanet is the primary listed proxy for bitcoin exposure in Tokyo and is deliberately plugging into Japan’s enormous savings and fixed-income base, I feel that the m/NAV compression reads like an opportunity. Lower entry multiples today, more diversified funding tomorrow, and a treasury that (so far) keeps getting bigger support the bullish story.
